essence: A capital-intensive, commodity, cyclical business where the global oil/gas price - largely outside any company's control - drives profitability.
Three distinct segments: upstream (find & produce, price-exposed), midstream (transport/store, fee-based, stable), downstream (refine & sell, margin = crack spread).
Reserves, cost-per-barrel, and access to low-cost resources are the core competitive position.
Heavily geopolitical and regulated; the energy transition is a structural long-term threat (and pivot opportunity).
Cash-flow-rich, but capital discipline and the transition (decarbonisation, value vs volume) define strategy.

definition.oneLiner: Companies that explore for, produce, transport, refine, and sell oil and natural gas - and the services around them.
definition.jobToBeDone: Supply the energy and feedstock that power transport, industry, heating, and petrochemicals - reliably and at scale.

valueChain.profitPools: Profit pools swing with the cycle: upstream captures the most at high prices (and bleeds at low ones); midstream is steadier (fee/toll-based); downstream profits on the crack spread and fuel retail. Low-cost producers and integrated majors capture value across the chain.
valueChain.powerShift: Value is gradually shifting from pure hydrocarbons -> gas/LNG + petrochemicals -> integrated energy (renewables, hydrogen) as the transition advances; near-term, capital discipline and shareholder returns dominate over volume growth.

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Revenue per barrel (or per mscf of gas); refining: margin per barrel (crack spread)
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Volume x Price. Volume = production/throughput; price = the global commodity price (largely exogenous) - so for upstream the oil price dominates; downstream depends on the crack spread.

costStructure.shape: Huge upfront capex (exploration, development, refineries) then relatively low operating (lifting) cost; profitability is dominated by the commodity price vs the cost-per-barrel - a low-cost producer survives any cycle.
costStructure.leverage: Operating leverage to the oil price is extreme - at high prices upstream gushes cash, at low prices high-cost producers lose money; the position-defining metric is breakeven cost per barrel.

competitionAndMoats.concentration: Dominated by national oil companies (Saudi Aramco etc.) + integrated supermajors (ExxonMobil, Shell, BP, TotalEnergies) + large independents; India: state-owned giants (ONGC, IOC, BPCL) + Reliance.

structuralForces.regulation: Heavy & geopolitical - licensing, royalties/taxes (large government take), environmental rules, fuel-pricing controls, OPEC supply management, and decarbonisation policy.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Among the most cyclical of all industries - driven by the global oil/gas price, OPEC decisions, geopolitics, and demand cycles.
structuralForces.substitution: The big structural threat: renewables, EVs, and electrification eroding oil demand long-term; gas as a transition fuel.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: OPEC and resource-holding governments control supply (huge power); oilfield-service providers; buyers are diffuse, but large industrial/utility buyers and governments (fuel pricing) matter.

aiPenetration.maturity: Medium - AI in exploration, operations, and predictive maintenance; trading is data-heavy.
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: AI improves the odds and cost of finding/producing (exploration, reservoir optimisation) and squeezes refinery/operations efficiency and uptime; it sharpens trading - but doesn't change the dominant exposure to the commodity price.
threatsDisruption: The energy transition (demand erosion) - the real disruptor, not AI itself

ranges: Breakeven oil price: the key survival metric (varies widely by basin)
Lifting cost: low for best basins (single-digit $/bbl) to high
Upstream margins: swing from huge to negative with price
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

valueLevers: Lower cost per barrel (operational efficiency, best basins)

keyTerms.Crack spread: The refining margin - difference between crude cost and product prices.
keyTerms.Lifting cost: Operating cost to extract a barrel.
keyTerms.Breakeven oil price: The oil price at which a project/company breaks even.
keyTerms.Reserve-replacement ratio: New reserves added vs produced - >100% sustains the business.
keyTerms.OPEC: Oil-producing countries' cartel that manages supply/price.
keyTerms.LNG: Liquefied Natural Gas - gas cooled for shipping.
keyTerms.Stranded asset: A reserve/asset that loses value as the transition advances.

questionsToAskFirst: What's the breakeven oil price / cost per barrel vs peers?
Upstream, midstream, or downstream exposure (or integrated)?
Balance-sheet strength to ride the cycle?
rookieTraps: Judging earnings at one point in a volatile price cycle

whatsUniqueHere: Oil & gas is the ultimate price-taker: a company can be brilliantly run and still lose money because the one variable that matters most - the global oil price - is set by OPEC, geopolitics, and demand far beyond its control. So the entire game reduces to cost per barrel (be the low-cost survivor) and capital discipline through violent cycles - all under the lengthening shadow of an energy transition that threatens to strand the very assets the business is built on.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: India is a huge, import-dependent energy consumer (imports ~85% of crude): state-owned giants dominate - ONGC (upstream), IOC/BPCL/HPCL (refining & fuel retail), GAIL (gas) - alongside Reliance (refining/petrochem) and a growing city-gas-distribution sector; fuel pricing is politically sensitive.
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

similarTo: Metals & mining - commodity, cyclical, asset-heavy, price-taking

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India oil & gas: heavy import reliance keeps it exposed to global price/geopolitics; state majors investing in refining, petrochemicals, and city-gas; Reliance integrating refining-to-chemicals and into new energy; gradual energy-transition pivots (biofuels, hydrogen, renewables) alongside continued fossil-demand growth.
